title: Inheritance & Dunder Methods
subtitle: Let your classes share superpowers and learn Python's secret magic methods.
goal: Build child classes that reuse a parent class, override methods, and use dunder methods like __str__, __eq__, and __len__ to make your objects feel like real Python.

blocks.heading: Classes can have family trees 🌳
blocks.html:
On Day 18 you built classes — blueprints for making objects. Today those blueprints learn to share. A child class can take everything a parent class already knows, then add its own twist.

Think about animals. A dog is an animal. A cat is an animal. They all eat and sleep like animals — but each one makes its own sound. We call this the "is-a" relationship, and Python calls it inheritance. 🧬

blocks.heading: Making a child class
blocks.html: To say "Dog is an Animal", you write the parent's name in parentheses: class Dog(Animal):. Now Dog gets every method and attribute Animal has — for free!

blocks.caption: Dog and Cat both inherit from Animal
blocks.explain: Dog and Cat never wrote their own __init__ or eat() — they borrowed them from Animal. But each one wrote its OWN speak(). Replacing a parent's method like this is called 'overriding'.

blocks.html: DRY = Don't Repeat Yourself. Inheritance means you write shared code once in the parent. Fix a bug there, and every child is fixed too. 🎉

blocks.heading: Calling the parent with super() 🦸
blocks.html:
Sometimes a child wants to add to the parent instead of fully replacing it. Use super() to run the parent's version first, then do your own extra step.

This is super handy in __init__ when the child needs extra info the parent doesn't know about.

blocks.caption: super() lets the child build on the parent
blocks.explain: super().__init__(name) runs Animal's __init__, so self.name gets set without copy-pasting that code. Then Dog adds self.breed on top.

blocks.question: What does class Cat(Animal): mean?
options: Cat is the parent of Animal
Cat inherits everything from Animal (Cat is-an Animal)
Cat and Animal are the exact same class
blocks.explain: Writing the parent in parentheses makes Cat a child of Animal. Cat gets all of Animal's methods and attributes, and can add or override its own.

blocks.question: Why would a child class call super().__init__()?
options: To run the parent's setup code so you don't repeat it
To make the program run faster
It is required in every method
blocks.explain: super().__init__() runs the parent's __init__, reusing its setup. Then the child can add its own extra attributes afterward.

blocks.title: Add a Bird that speaks
blocks.instructions: A parent Animal class is ready. Finish the Bird child class so it inherits from Animal and overrides speak() to print a tweet. Then make a Bird and call speak(). Make sure the output contains the word Tweet.
blocks.passMsg: Tweet tweet! Your Bird inherited from Animal and got its own voice. 🐦
blocks.failMsg: Make Bird inherit with class Bird(Animal):, override speak() to print 'Tweet', then create a Bird and call speak().

blocks.heading: Dunder methods: Python's magic ✨
blocks.html:
Some method names start and end with two underscores, like __init__. Coders call these dunder methods ("double underscore"). They're magic hooks Python calls automatically at the right moment.

You already met __init__ — it runs when you create an object. There are many more, and they let your objects behave like Python's built-in types.

blocks.heading: __str__ makes print() friendly 🖨️
blocks.html: By default, printing an object shows something ugly like <__main__.Circle object at 0x...>. Add a __str__ method that returns a string, and print() will use it instead. There's also __repr__, a more technical "for programmers" version Python shows in places like lists.

blocks.caption: Shapes with __str__, __repr__, and __eq__
blocks.explain: __str__ gives a friendly message for print(). __eq__ lets you compare two objects with ==, deciding what 'equal' means (here: same radius). __repr__ is the developer-friendly version Python shows inside a list.

blocks.html: Watch out! __str__ and __repr__ must return a string, not print it. Use return, and wrap numbers in str(...) before joining them with +.

blocks.heading: __len__ teaches len() about your object 📏
blocks.html: Ever used len("hello") to get 5? You can teach len() to work on YOUR objects too by adding a __len__ method that returns a number.

blocks.caption: A Team object that knows its own length
blocks.explain: Because Team has __len__, calling len(lions) returns 3. Python's built-in len() looks for a __len__ method on the object and uses it.

blocks.question: Which dunder method does print(my_object) use to show a friendly message?
blocks.explain: print() calls __str__ if you defined it. Without it, you get the ugly default like <__main__.Circle object at 0x...>.

blocks.title: Give Book a nice printout and a length
blocks.instructions: Add a __str__ method to Book that returns a string like 'Matilda by Roald Dahl', and a __len__ method that returns the number of pages. Then print the book and print its length.
blocks.starter:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages

    # add __str__ here (must return a string)

    # add __len__ here (must return self.pages)

b = Book("Matilda", "Roald Dahl", 240)
print(b)
print(len(b))
blocks.passMsg: Your Book prints beautifully and len() works on it. That's dunder magic! 📚
blocks.failMsg: Make __str__ return self.title + ' by ' + self.author, and make __len__ return self.pages. Both must use return, not print.

challenge.title: Build a Shape family with super() and dunders
challenge.instructions: Create a parent class Shape that stores a name in __init__. Then make two children: Square(side) and Rectangle(width, height). Each should call super().__init__() with its name, have an area() method, and a __str__ that says the name and its area. Create one of each, print them, and print their areas. Your output should contain the words Square and Rectangle.
challenge.hint: Each child's __init__ should call super().__init__("Square") or super().__init__("Rectangle") first, then store its own sizes. __str__ must return a string, so wrap area() in str().
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Incredible! You built a class family that shares code with super() AND speaks Python's magic dunder language. Day 19 conquered!
